claims.normalized_claim: In Riley v. California (2014) the Supreme Court held that police generally may not, without a warrant, search the digital contents of a cell phone seized from an individual who has been arrested.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (Roberts, C.J.), holding
allowed_wording: Police generally need a warrant to search a cell phone seized during an arrest.
prohibited_wording: Police can never look at your phone.

claims.normalized_claim: Riley v. California was decided on June 25, 2014; the judgment was unanimous (9-0), with the majority opinion by Chief Justice John Roberts and Justice Alito concurring in part and in the judgment.
allowed_wording: A unanimous Court ruled in 2014; Chief Justice Roberts wrote the opinion.
prohibited_wording: The opinion was joined in full by every justice.
claims.notes: 9-0 in the result; Alito concurred in part and in the judgment (not full reasoning).

claims.normalized_claim: The Court held that the traditional 'search incident to arrest' justifications from Chimel v. California — officer safety and preventing the destruction of evidence — do not justify searching the digital data on a phone.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, application of Chimel rationales
allowed_wording: The usual reasons for searching an arrestee don't fit phone data.
prohibited_wording: The Court abolished search incident to arrest.
claims.notes: Reasoning: data can't be a weapon; secured phone removes destruction concern.

claims.normalized_claim: The Court reasoned that modern cell phones differ from other objects 'in both a quantitative and a qualitative sense,' holding what it called the privacies of life.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (quantitative/qualitative difference; 'privacies of life')
allowed_wording: The Court said a phone is different in kind, holding 'the privacies of life.'

claims.normalized_claim: Riley's case was decided together with United States v. Wurie, which involved an older flip phone whose call log police used without a warrant.
allowed_wording: The case was decided alongside a second one about an old flip phone, United States v. Wurie.

claims.normalized_claim: David Riley was stopped in San Diego while driving on a suspended license; firearms were found in his car; officers searched his smartphone without a warrant (at the scene and again at the station), and photos, videos and contacts on it linked him to a street gang and an earlier shooting, leading to charges in that shooting.
allowed_wording: Riley was stopped, guns were found, his phone was searched without a warrant, and it tied him to a shooting.
prohibited_wording: The phone search proved him guilty.
claims.notes: Real person; charged status accurate; do not editorialize on guilt.

claims.normalized_claim: The Court's instruction to officers who want to search a phone was, in its words, 'get a warrant.'
allowed_wording: The Court's answer was simple: get a warrant.

claims.normalized_claim: The ruling preserves the exigent-circumstances exception, allowing a warrantless phone search in a genuine emergency (such as imminent destruction of evidence, a fleeing suspect, or someone in serious danger).
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, exigency discussion
allowed_wording: In a true emergency, police can still act without a warrant.

claims.normalized_claim: Riley builds on the principle from Katz v. United States (1967) that the Fourth Amendment 'protects people, not places,' tied to a person's reasonable expectation of privacy.
evidence_locations: Katz, Opinion of the Court ('people, not places')
Katz, Harlan, J. concurrence (reasonable expectation of privacy)
allowed_wording: The older principle that privacy follows the person, not the place, comes from Katz (1967).
claims.notes: Background principle woven in; sourced to the Katz primary opinion.

findings: Durable content hashes captured for 2/3 sources; SRC-0002 returned a fetch block (403) — non-blocking, primary opinion(s) hashed.
